rules.title: Don't Forget the Accusative Case for Direct Objects
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the direct object (the noun receiving the action) must be in the accusative case, not the nominative. English speakers often forget to change feminine nouns ending in -a to -u, and for animate masculine nouns, add an -a. This is one of the most common mistakes with cases.

examples.note: Feminine noun 'kuća' changes to 'kuću' in accusative.
Animate masculine noun 'Marko' gets an -a in accusative.
Another feminine noun 'knjiga' changes to 'knjigu'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I drink water' in Serbian? (water = voda)
Translate: 'He has a cat.' (cat = mačka)
Correct the mistake: 'Vidim Ivan.' (I see Ivan)

rules.title: Serbian Verbal Aspect: Perfective vs. Imperfective
rules.explanation: In Serbian, verbs have two aspects: imperfective (for ongoing or repeated actions) and perfective (for completed or one-time actions). English learners often confuse them because English doesn’t have a built-in aspect system. A common mistake is using the perfective where an imperfective is needed, or vice versa.

examples.wrong: Juče sam pročitao knjigu kad je on došao.
examples.right: Juče sam čitao knjigu kad je on došao.
examples.note: Use imperfective 'čitao' to describe an ongoing action interrupted by 'došao' (perfective).
Use imperfective 'pijem' for habitual actions; perfective 'popijem' would mean 'drink (and finish)' once.

examples.wrong: On je napisao pismo kada sam ga zvao.
examples.right: On je pisao pismo kada sam ga zvao.
examples.note: Use imperfective 'pisao' to show the letter was in progress when the call happened.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I am reading a book now.' Use the correct aspect.
Translate: 'Yesterday I read (finished) a book.' Use the correct aspect.
Fill in the blank: 'On ________ (piše / napiše) pismo svaki dan.'

rules.title: Correct Stress in Serbian Words
rules.explanation: In Serbian, every word has a stressed syllable, but the stress is not indicated in writing. Misplacing the stress can make words sound unnatural or even change their meaning. A common mistake for English speakers is to stress the wrong syllable, often following English patterns.

examples.note: The correct stress is on the first syllable ('шко'), not on the second ('ко').
The stress is on the second syllable ('ди'), not on the first ('ра') as in English.
Stress the first syllable ('мле'), not the second ('ко').

exercises.prompt: How do you correctly stress the Serbian word for 'school'?
Which syllable is stressed in the word for 'milk'? Write the word with correct stress.
Correct the stress in this word: 'радио' (stressed on the first syllable). What is the correct form?

rules.title: Adjective-Noun Gender Agreement in Serbian
rules.explanation: In Serbian, adjectives must match the gender (masculine, feminine, or neuter) of the noun they describe. A common mistake is using the default masculine form for all nouns. The adjective ending changes: masculine usually ends in a consonant, feminine adds -a, and neuter adds -o or -e.

examples.note: "Kuća" is feminine, so the adjective must be "lepa" (not the masculine "lep").
"Čovek" is masculine, so the adjective is "lep" (not the neuter "lepo").
"Dete" is neuter, so the adjective is "lepo" (not the feminine "lepa").

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct form of 'nov' (new) for the feminine noun 'knjiga' (book).
Fill in the correct form of 'star' (old) for the neuter noun 'auto' (car).
Fill in the correct form of 'dobar' (good) for the masculine noun 'prijatelj' (friend).

rules.title: Soft Consonants (Меки сугласници)
rules.explanation: In Serbian, some consonants have a 'soft' (palatalized) version, pronounced with the tongue raised toward the palate. Mixing them with their 'hard' counterparts is a common mistake and can change meaning. For example, 'ћ' (soft) vs. 'ч' (hard), 'ђ' (soft) vs. 'џ' (hard), and 'љ' (soft) vs. 'л' (hard) are distinct sounds.

examples.note: Need soft 'ћ' for 'house', not hard 'ч'.
Soft 'ђ' means 'student'; hard 'џ' means 'sack'.
Soft 'љ' is required for 'love'.

exercises.prompt: Write the Serbian word for 'night' (soft consonant).
Write the Serbian word for 'student' (soft consonant).
Write the Serbian word for 'love' (soft consonant).

rules.title: Intonation in Serbian Questions
rules.explanation: In Serbian, yes/no questions require a rising intonation on the final syllable, unlike English which often uses a rising pattern at the end. A common mistake is using a flat or falling tone, making the question sound like a statement. Practicing the rise on the last word is key to sounding natural.

examples.note: The period indicates a flat tone; the question mark implies the necessary rising intonation on 'došao'.
Rise on 'gladan' distinguishes a question from a statement.

exercises.prompt: Convert this statement into a yes/no question with correct intonation: 'Ti si umoran.'
Add the correct intonation (punctuation) to make this a question: 'Ona voli muziku'

rules.title: Clitic Pronoun Placement in Serbian
rules.explanation: In Serbian, clitic pronouns (e.g., ga, je, mu, se) must occupy the second position in a sentence, after the first stressed word or phrase. A common mistake is placing them first or after the verb. Remember: the clitic cannot start a clause.

examples.note: The clitic 'ga' cannot be first. Move it after the verb.
The auxiliary 'sam' is also a clitic; place it after 'Ja', then the pronoun 'je'.
This is actually correct! 'Mu' is in second position (after 'Dajem'). If you placed it first, it would be wrong.

exercises.prompt: Correct the clitic placement: 'Se radujem.' (meaning 'I am pleased.')
Correct the clitic placement: 'Knjigu mu dajem.' (meaning 'I give him the book.')
Correct the clitic placement: 'Je ona lepa.' (meaning 'She is beautiful.')

rules.title: Using 'se' with Transitive Verbs
rules.explanation: In Serbian, many transitive verbs require the reflexive particle 'se' when the subject performs the action on itself. English speakers often forget this 'se' because English does not use it the same way. Remember: if the action is done to oneself, add 'se' after the verb (or before it in certain tenses).

examples.note: 'Prati' (to wash) needs 'se' when washing oneself, not just washing something.
'Oblačiti' (to dress) requires 'se' when dressing oneself; without it, it means dressing someone else.
'Brijati' (to shave) needs 'se' for shaving oneself.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I get up' (literally 'I raise myself')?
Translate 'She combs her hair' (meaning combs herself).
How do you say 'We hide' (ourselves)?

rules.title: Verbs with Required Prepositions
rules.explanation: In Serbian, many verbs require specific prepositions that differ from English. For example, 'to wait for' is simply 'čekati' (no preposition), while 'to think about' is 'misliti na' (not 'misliti o'). Using the wrong preposition is a common mistake for English speakers.

examples.note: Čekati takes a direct object, no preposition.
Misliti na + accusative means 'think of/about'. Misliti o means 'have an opinion about'.
Tražiti takes a direct object, no preposition.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am waiting for the bus' in Serbian?
How do you say 'She is thinking about her friend' in Serbian?
How do you say 'He is looking for his keys' in Serbian?

rules.title: Past Tense Auxiliary Agreement
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the past tense is formed with the present tense of the auxiliary verb 'biti' (sam, si, je, smo, ste, su) plus the past participle. The auxiliary must match the subject in person and number. A common mistake is using 'je' (3rd person singular) for plural subjects or incorrect forms for other persons.

examples.note: Use 'su' for plural subjects (they). 'Je' is only for singular third person (he/she/it).
Use 'smo' for 'we' (first person plural).

exercises.prompt: Correct the auxiliary: 'Ja je bio.'
Fill in the correct auxiliary: 'Vi ______ radili.'
Choose the right auxiliary: 'One (je/su) bile.'

rules.title: Future Tense: 'ću' vs 'bude'
rules.explanation: In Serbian, 'ću' is the auxiliary verb for forming the future tense (future I) meaning 'I will'. 'Bude' is the present tense of 'biti' used only in specific constructions like future II or conditional clauses. A common mistake is using 'bude' instead of 'ću' to express simple future actions.

examples.note: Use 'ću' for simple future (I will come). 'Bude' is not used here.
'Će' is the third-person singular form of the auxiliary. 'Bude' would be incorrect.
For 'we will travel', use 'ćemo' (first person plural).

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I will buy bread.' (Use correct future auxiliary)
Correct this sentence: 'Ti bude učiti srpski.'
Fill in the blank: 'Oni _____ doći u petak.' (use proper future form)

rules.title: Mastering the Conditional 'bih' Forms
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the conditional auxiliary changes depending on the subject. The form 'bih' is only used for the first person singular (ja). Common mistakes include using 'bih' for all persons, when it should be 'bi' for second/third singular, 'bismo' for first plural, 'biste' for second plural, and 'bi' for third plural.

examples.note: Use 'bi' for second person singular (ti), not 'bih'.
Use 'bi' for third person plural (oni), not 'bih'.
Use 'bismo' for first person plural (mi).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct conditional auxiliary: Ja ___ došao.
Fill in the correct conditional auxiliary: Ti ___ došla.
Fill in the correct conditional auxiliary: Mi ___ došli.

rules.title: Irregular Imperatives in Serbian: Common Mistakes
rules.explanation: In Serbian, some verbs have irregular imperative forms that do not follow the regular pattern of adding -j or -i to the infinitive stem. A common mistake is using the infinitive form instead of the correct imperative. For example, the verb 'ići' (to go) becomes 'idi' (go!), not 'ići'. Learning these irregular forms will help you sound more natural.

examples.note: The imperative of 'ići' is 'idi' for the singular 'ti' form.
The imperative of 'doći' is 'dođi', not the infinitive.
For 'reći', the imperative is 'reci' (drop the infinitive ending and change the stem).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Eat!' (informal singular) from the verb 'jesti'?
How do you say 'Take!' (informal singular) from the verb 'uzeti'?
How do you say 'Be!' (informal singular) from the verb 'biti'?

rules.title: Numbers 1-4: Gender Agreement
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the numbers 1 (jedan), 2 (dva/dve), 3 (tri), and 4 (četiri) must agree in gender with the noun they count. A common mistake is using the masculine form for feminine nouns (e.g., 'dva knjige' instead of 'dve knjige'). For 1, use 'jedan' (masculine), 'jedna' (feminine), 'jedno' (neuter); for 2, use 'dva' (masculine/neuter) and 'dve' (feminine); 3 and 4 stay the same for all genders.

examples.note: 'Knjige' is feminine plural, so use 'dve', not 'dva'.
'Soba' is feminine, so use 'jedna' instead of 'jedan'.
Correct as is – 'tri' works for all genders.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct form of '1' (jedan/jedna/jedno): 'Ovo je ___ stolica.'
Fill in the blank with the correct form of '2' (dva/dve): 'Imam ___ prijatelja.'
Fill in the blank with the correct form of '3' (tri): 'Kupila je ___ jabuke.'

rules.title: Gender of Nouns Ending in -a
rules.explanation: In Serbian, most nouns ending in -a are feminine. However, nouns that refer to male people (e.g., tata 'dad', kolega 'colleague', sudija 'judge') are masculine. This means adjectives, possessive pronouns, and numerals must agree with the masculine gender, not the feminine ending. Always check the meaning to determine the correct gender.

examples.note: Even though 'tata' ends in -a, it is masculine, so use the masculine adjective 'lep'.
'kolega' is masculine, so the possessive must be 'moj' (masculine), not 'moja' (feminine).
Numerals also agree in gender: 'jednog' (masculine accusative) is correct for 'sudija'.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct possessive: ___ (Moj/Moja) tata je inženjer.
Choose the correct adjective: Ona je (dobar/dobra) sudija.
Fill in: Ovo je ___ (jedan/jedna) kolega.

rules.title: Possessive 'svoj' vs 'moj'
rules.explanation: 'Svoj' is a reflexive possessive pronoun meaning 'one's own' and must refer back to the subject of the sentence. 'Moj' is a non-reflexive possessive meaning 'my'. A common mistake is using 'moj' when the subject is the same as the possessor, but 'svoj' should be used instead.

examples.note: Use 'svoju' because the subject 'ja' possesses the family.
This is correct already: 'svoj' matches the subject 'on'.
If Marija bought her own book, use 'svoju'; 'moju' would mean she bought my book.

exercises.prompt: Fill in: I wash _____ hands. (my own)
Fill in: He loves _____ dog. (his own)
Fill in: She finished _____ homework. (her own)

rules.title: Demonstratives: ovaj, taj, onaj
rules.explanation: In Serbian, ovaj means 'this' (close to the speaker), taj means 'that' (close to the listener or already known), and onaj means 'that over there' (far from both). A common mistake is using ovaj for something far or using taj when pointing to something close.

examples.note: Use onaj for something far away, not ovaj.

examples.wrong: Daj mi onu knjigu koju držiš.
examples.right: Daj mi tu knjigu koju držiš.
examples.note: Use taj/tu for something near the listener, not onaj.

exercises.prompt: Fill in: ___ (this, near me) stolica je udobna.
Fill in: ___ (that, near you) olovka je tvoja?
Fill in: ___ (that over there) planina je visoka.

rules.title: Choosing the Right Question Word: Šta, Ko, Koji
rules.explanation: In Serbian, 'šta' means 'what' and is used for things or general information. 'Ko' means 'who' and is only for people. 'Koji' means 'which' and is used when you are choosing from a known set of options (it agrees in gender and number with the noun it refers to).

examples.note: Use 'ko' for people, not 'šta'.
'Koji' must agree in case (accusative feminine singular here).

examples.wrong: Ko je ovo? (pointing to a cat)
examples.right: Šta je ovo? (pointing to a cat)
examples.note: Use 'šta' for animals/objects, 'ko' for people.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'Who is calling?'
Translate: 'What is this book about?'
Translate: 'Which dress do you like?' (dress = haljina, feminine singular)

rules.title: Choosing the Right Conjunction: a, ali, nego, pa
rules.explanation: In Serbian, these four conjunctions are often confused. Use 'ali' for a direct, strong contrast (like 'but'). Use 'a' for a milder contrast or to add new information. Use 'nego' after a negative statement to mean 'but rather'. Use 'pa' to show sequence or result ('and then', 'and so').

examples.wrong: Želeo sam da dođem, a nisam mogao.
examples.right: Želeo sam da dođem, ali nisam mogao.
examples.note: Use 'ali' for a strong contrast or obstacle; 'a' would be too weak here.

examples.wrong: Bio je umoran, pa je nastavio da radi.
examples.right: Bio je umoran, ali je nastavio da radi.
examples.note: Use 'ali' to express 'although he was tired, he continued'; 'pa' would mean 'so he continued' (result).
After a negative clause, use 'nego' (not 'ali') to say 'but rather'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct conjunction: 'Nisam video film, ____ sam čitao knjigu.' (after negation)
exercises.answer: Nisam video film, nego sam čitao knjigu.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'Prvo sam se probudio, ____ sam doručkovao.' (sequence of actions)
exercises.answer: Prvo sam se probudio, pa sam doručkovao.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: 'Ona voli kafu, ____ on voli čaj.' (mild contrast / adding info)
exercises.answer: Ona voli kafu, a on voli čaj.

rules.title: Comparative of Adverbs: A Common Mistake
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the comparative of adverbs is formed with a single word (e.g., brzo → brže), not by adding 'više' (more). Another common error is using the adjective form (which agrees in gender/number) instead of the adverb when describing an action. Remember: adverbs are invariable.

examples.note: Do not use 'više' + adverb; use the synthetic comparative form.
Adverbs do not change for gender; use the neuter comparative ending.
Irregular adverbs like 'dobro' have a special comparative form: 'bolje'.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the comparative: On uči (mnogo) ______.
Complete with the comparative: Ona priča (glasno) ______.
Complete with the comparative: Oni rade (dobro) ______.

rules.title: Verbs of Motion Prefixes: Doći vs. Otići, Ući vs. Izići
rules.explanation: In Serbian, prefixes attached to verbs of motion change the direction or type of movement. Common pairs are 'do-' (arrive/come) vs. 'od-' (depart/go away), and 'u-' (enter) vs. 'iz-' (exit). English often uses the same verb 'go' or 'come' without these distinctions, which leads to mistakes.

examples.note: 'Doći' means to arrive/come, while 'otići' means to leave/go away. Use 'doći' when arriving at a place.
Use 'otići' when leaving a place, not 'doći'.
'Ući' means to enter, 'izići' means to exit. The prefix 'iz-' indicates movement out of a place.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'He came home'?
How do you say 'She left the room'?
How do you say 'I went to the store' (meaning I departed from home and went there)?

rules.title: Forming Yes/No Questions with 'li'
rules.explanation: In Serbian, you cannot simply raise your intonation to turn a statement into a yes/no question. Instead, you must add the particle 'li' after the verb (or the auxiliary verb). The most common pattern is 'Da li' + subject + verb, but you can also attach 'li' directly to the verb (e.g., 'Jesi li...'). Remember that 'li' always comes right after the first verb in the question.

examples.note: The statement 'Ti si student' becomes a question by adding 'Da li' at the beginning. 'Da li' is the standard, neutral way to form yes/no questions.
Again, simply adding a question mark is incorrect. Use 'Da li' before the subject and verb.

exercises.prompt: Ask 'Are you going to the store?' using 'Da li'.
Ask 'Does she speak English?' using 'Da li'.

rules.title: Double Negation in Serbian
rules.explanation: In Serbian, when a sentence is negative, all relevant words (like 'no one', 'nothing', 'never') must also be in a negative form. This is called double (or multiple) negation. Unlike English, Serbian requires every negative element to appear as a negative word, not just one.

examples.note: Actually this is correct Serbian—both 'niko' (no one) and 'nije' (is not) are negative. A common mistake: using 'Neko nije došao' (someone didn't come) instead of the proper double negative.
This is correct. A typical error is 'Ne znam nešto' (I don't know something) instead of using 'ništa' (nothing).
Correct. Wrong would be 'Nikad ću zaboraviti' (I will never forget) without the negative auxiliary 'neću'.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'Nobody called me.' (Use double negation)
Translate: 'I never go there.' (Use double negation)
Translate: 'I don't see anything.' (Use double negation)

rules.title: Word Order in Subordinate Clauses
rules.explanation: In Serbian, the verb in a subordinate clause usually comes at the end of the clause, unlike English where it follows the subject. This is a common mistake for English speakers. Remember to place the verb last after conjunctions like 'da' (that), 'kada' (when), or 'ako' (if).

examples.note: The verb 'voli' moves to the end after 'da'.

examples.wrong: Kada sam došao, ona je spavala.
examples.right: Kada sam došao, ona spavala je.
examples.note: In the 'kada' clause, the auxiliary 'je' goes after the main verb 'spavala'.

examples.wrong: Ako on hoće da ide, idemo.
examples.right: Ako on da ide hoće, idemo.
examples.note: Both verbs in the 'ako' clause follow the end-of-clause rule: 'hoće' after 'da ide'.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I think that she reads books.' (Use 'Mislim da...')
Correct the word order: 'Kada on dolazi, mi smo srećni.'
exercises.answer: Kada on dolazi, mi srećni smo.

exercises.prompt: Finish the sentence: 'Ako ti (to radiš)....' (Place the verb correctly.)
